packs.name: AWS API Gateway
packs.description: Protects against destructive AWS API Gateway CLI operations for both REST APIs and HTTP APIs.

structural_patterns.name: apigateway-delete-rest-api
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigateway delete-rest-api permanently removes a REST API and all its resources.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a REST API removes all resources, methods, stages, deployments, and configurations. All clients will immediately receive errors. API keys and usage plans referencing this API become orphaned but are not automatically deleted.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigateway get-rest-api: Review API details before deletion
- aws apigateway get-stages: Check active stages and traffic
- Export API definition with aws apigateway get-export first

structural_patterns.name: apigateway-delete-resource
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigateway delete-resource removes an API resource and its methods.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a resource removes the URL path and all HTTP methods defined on it. Clients calling that endpoint will receive 404 errors. Child resources are also deleted recursively.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigateway get-resources: Review resource tree first
- Deploy to a test stage to verify the change
- Delete individual methods instead if only removing specific operations

structural_patterns.name: apigateway-delete-method
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigateway delete-method removes an HTTP method from a resource.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a method removes the HTTP operation (GET, POST, etc.) from a resource. Clients calling that method will receive 404 or 405 errors after redeployment.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigateway get-method: Review method configuration first
- Test changes in a non-production stage
- Consider disabling the method instead of deleting

structural_patterns.name: apigateway-delete-stage
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigateway delete-stage removes a deployment stage from an API.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a stage stops all traffic to that deployment. Stage variables, caching settings, and throttling configurations are lost. Clients using the stage URL will receive errors immediately.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigateway get-stage: Review stage settings first
- Redirect traffic to another stage before deletion
- Export stage configuration for backup

structural_patterns.name: apigateway-delete-deployment
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigateway delete-deployment removes a deployment from an API.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a deployment removes a specific API snapshot. If the deployment is currently active on a stage, that stage becomes unavailable. Rollback to this version becomes impossible after deletion.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigateway get-deployments: List deployments first
- Ensure no stages reference this deployment
- Keep recent deployments for rollback capability

structural_patterns.name: apigateway-delete-api-key
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigateway delete-api-key removes an API key.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an API key immediately revokes access for any client using that key. Requests will be rejected with 403 Forbidden. Usage tracking history for the key is preserved but the key cannot be recovered.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigateway get-api-key: Review key details first
- Disable the key instead of deleting to preserve for audit
- Notify affected clients before deletion

structural_patterns.name: apigateway-delete-authorizer
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigateway delete-authorizer removes an authorizer from an API.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an authorizer breaks authentication for all methods using it. Those methods will fail authorization until reconfigured. Lambda authorizer functions are not deleted but become orphaned.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigateway get-authorizers: Review authorizers first
- Update methods to use a different authorizer before deletion
- Test in a non-production stage first

structural_patterns.name: apigateway-delete-model
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigateway delete-model removes a model from an API.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a model removes the JSON schema definition. Methods referencing this model for request/response validation will lose that validation. The API will still function but without schema enforcement.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigateway get-models: Review models first
- Check which methods reference this model
- Update method configurations to remove model references first

structural_patterns.name: apigateway-delete-domain-name
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigateway delete-domain-name removes a custom domain name.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a custom domain name breaks all traffic using that domain. The ACM certificate is not deleted but becomes unused. DNS records pointing to the domain will fail to resolve API traffic.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigateway get-domain-names: Review domains first
- Update DNS records before domain deletion
- Verify no production traffic uses this domain

structural_patterns.name: apigateway-delete-usage-plan
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigateway delete-usage-plan removes a usage plan.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a usage plan removes throttling and quota limits for associated API keys. Keys lose their rate limiting, which may cause backend overload or unexpected billing. Key associations are removed.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigateway get-usage-plans: Review plans first
- aws apigateway get-usage-plan-keys: Check associated keys
- Migrate keys to another plan before deletion

structural_patterns.name: apigatewayv2-delete-api
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigatewayv2 delete-api permanently removes an HTTP API.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an HTTP API removes all routes, integrations, stages, and configurations. All clients will immediately lose access. WebSocket connections are terminated. The API ID cannot be reused.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigatewayv2 get-api: Review API details first
- aws apigatewayv2 export-api: Export OpenAPI spec for backup
- Verify no production traffic before deletion

structural_patterns.name: apigatewayv2-delete-route
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigatewayv2 delete-route removes a route from an HTTP API.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a route removes the path and method combination from the API. Clients calling that endpoint will receive 404 errors. Route authorization settings and request validation are also removed.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigatewayv2 get-routes: List routes first
- Test in $default stage before production
- Consider updating the route instead of deleting

structural_patterns.name: apigatewayv2-delete-integration
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigatewayv2 delete-integration removes an integration from an HTTP API.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an integration breaks routes using it. Those routes will fail to invoke backend services. Lambda function configurations, HTTP endpoints, and VPC link settings are lost.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigatewayv2 get-integrations: Review integrations first
- Check which routes use this integration
- Update routes to use a different integration first

structural_patterns.name: apigatewayv2-delete-stage
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigatewayv2 delete-stage removes a stage from an HTTP API.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a stage stops all traffic to that deployment. Stage variables, access logs, throttling, and auto-deploy settings are lost. Clients using the stage URL will receive errors.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigatewayv2 get-stages: Review stages first
- Redirect traffic before deletion
- Keep $default stage for production traffic

structural_patterns.name: apigatewayv2-delete-authorizer
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigatewayv2 delete-authorizer removes an authorizer from an HTTP API.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an authorizer breaks authentication for routes using it. JWT validation and Lambda authorization will fail. Routes must be updated to remove or replace the authorizer reference.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigatewayv2 get-authorizers: Review authorizers first
- Update routes to use a different authorizer
- Test authorization changes in a test stage

structural_patterns.name: apigatewayv2-delete-domain-name
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigatewayv2 delete-domain-name removes a custom domain name from an HTTP API.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a custom domain breaks traffic using that hostname. API mappings are removed. DNS records will fail to route to the API. The ACM certificate remains but becomes unused.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigatewayv2 get-domain-names: Review domains first
- Update DNS before deletion
- Remove API mappings first to verify impact

structural_patterns.name: apigatewayv2-delete-route-response
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigatewayv2 delete-route-response removes a route response from an HTTP API.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a route response removes response configuration for WebSocket APIs. Response selection expressions and model mappings are lost. Clients may receive unexpected response formats.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigatewayv2 get-route-responses: Review responses first
- Test in a non-production stage
- Update response instead of deleting

structural_patterns.name: apigatewayv2-delete-integration-response
structural_patterns.reason: aws apigatewayv2 delete-integration-response removes an integration response.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an integration response removes response mapping for WebSocket APIs. Template transformations and response codes are lost. Clients may receive raw backend responses.

Safer alternatives:
- aws apigatewayv2 get-integration-responses: Review first
- Test response handling in a test stage
- Update the response instead of deleting

packs.name: Kong API Gateway
packs.description: Protects against destructive Kong Gateway CLI, deck CLI, and Admin API operations.

structural_patterns.name: deck-reset
structural_patterns.reason: deck reset removes ALL Kong configuration. This is extremely dangerous and irreversible.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Resetting Kong removes ALL services, routes, plugins, consumers, certificates, and upstreams. Your entire API gateway configuration is wiped. All traffic through Kong will fail immediately. There is no undo.

Safer alternatives:
- deck dump: Export current configuration first
- deck diff: Review what will change before any sync
- Delete specific entities instead of full reset

structural_patterns.name: deck-sync-destructive
structural_patterns.reason: deck sync with --select-tag can remove entities not matching the tag.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Using --select-tag with deck sync removes entities not matching the tag in your state file. This can accidentally delete services and routes managed by other teams or systems. Entities without tags are at risk of deletion.

Safer alternatives:
- deck diff --select-tag: Preview changes first
- Use consistent tagging across all entities
- Consider deck sync without --select-tag to avoid surprises

structural_patterns.name: kong-admin-delete-services
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Kong Admin API removes services.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Kong service removes the upstream service definition. All routes associated with the service become orphaned and stop working. Traffic to those endpoints will return 404 errors.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /services first: List services to verify the target
- Delete routes pointing to the service first
- Use deck dump to export configuration before changes

structural_patterns.name: kong-admin-delete-routes
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Kong Admin API removes routes.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Kong route removes the path-to-service mapping. Requests to that path will return 404 errors. Plugins attached to the route are also removed. The associated service is not affected.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /routes first: Verify the route details
- Test in a non-production environment
- Consider disabling plugins instead of deleting the route

structural_patterns.name: kong-admin-delete-plugins
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Kong Admin API removes plugins.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Kong plugin removes its functionality from the associated service, route, or consumer. Authentication, rate limiting, logging, or transformation features provided by the plugin stop immediately.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /plugins first: Review plugin configuration
- PATCH to disable the plugin instead of deleting
- Export plugin config with deck dump before deletion

structural_patterns.name: kong-admin-delete-consumers
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Kong Admin API removes consumers.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Kong consumer removes the API client identity. All credentials (API keys, JWT, OAuth) for that consumer are revoked. Plugins configured per-consumer stop applying. Affected clients lose API access.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /consumers first: Review consumer details
- Delete individual credentials instead of the consumer
- Notify affected clients before deletion

structural_patterns.name: kong-admin-delete-upstreams
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Kong Admin API removes upstreams.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Kong upstream removes the load balancer and all its targets. Services using this upstream will fail to route traffic. Health checks and circuit breaker settings are lost.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /upstreams first: Review upstream configuration
- Update services to use a different upstream first
- Remove targets individually to verify impact

structural_patterns.name: kong-admin-delete-targets
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Kong Admin API removes targets.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Kong target removes a backend server from the upstream pool. Traffic is redistributed to remaining targets. If this was the last target, the upstream has no backends and requests fail.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /upstreams/{id}/targets first: List current targets
- Set target weight to 0 to drain traffic first
- Verify other targets can handle the load

structural_patterns.name: kong-admin-delete-certificates
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Kong Admin API removes certificates.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Kong certificate removes the TLS/SSL certificate. SNIs using this certificate will fail TLS handshakes. HTTPS traffic to affected domains will receive certificate errors.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /certificates first: Review certificate details
- Upload a replacement certificate before deletion
- Remove associated SNIs first

structural_patterns.name: kong-admin-delete-snis
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Kong Admin API removes SNIs.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Kong SNI removes the domain-to-certificate mapping. HTTPS requests for that hostname will fail TLS negotiation or use a default certificate if configured. Clients will see certificate warnings.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /snis first: Review SNI configuration
- Update the SNI to point to a different certificate
- Verify the certificate is no longer needed for this domain

structural_patterns.name: kong-admin-delete-generic
structural_patterns.reason: DELETE request to Kong Admin API can remove configuration.
structural_patterns.explanation:
DELETE requests to the Kong Admin API remove configuration objects. The specific impact depends on the endpoint: services, routes, plugins, consumers, certificates, or other Kong entities. Deletions take effect immediately.

Safer alternatives:
- Use GET requests first to review the resource
- Export configuration with deck dump
- Test changes in a staging environment

packs.name: Google Apigee
packs.description: Protects against destructive Google Apigee CLI and apigeecli operations.

structural_patterns.name: gcloud-apigee-apis-delete
structural_patterns.reason: gcloud apigee apis delete removes an API proxy from Apigee.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an API proxy removes all its revisions and deployment history. Any applications relying on this API will immediately receive errors. API keys and quotas associated with products referencing this API may be affected.

Safer alternatives:
- gcloud apigee apis list: Review APIs before deletion
- gcloud apigee deployments undeploy: Undeploy first to verify impact
- Export the API proxy bundle before deletion for backup

structural_patterns.name: gcloud-apigee-environments-delete
structural_patterns.reason: gcloud apigee environments delete removes an Apigee environment.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an environment removes all deployed API proxies, target servers, key-value maps, and caches within it. This is irreversible and will break all API traffic routed through this environment.

Safer alternatives:
- gcloud apigee environments describe: Review environment contents
- Undeploy all APIs from the environment first
- Export environment configuration and resources before deletion

structural_patterns.name: gcloud-apigee-developers-delete
structural_patterns.reason: gcloud apigee developers delete removes a developer from Apigee.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a developer also deletes all their apps and associated API keys. Any applications using those keys will immediately lose API access. Developer analytics and usage history are also removed.

Safer alternatives:
- gcloud apigee developers describe: Review developer details
- Revoke individual app credentials instead of deleting the developer
- Set developer status to inactive rather than deleting

structural_patterns.name: gcloud-apigee-products-delete
structural_patterns.reason: gcloud apigee products delete removes an API product from Apigee.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an API product immediately revokes access for all apps subscribed to it. Quota settings, rate limits, and access controls defined in the product are lost. Apps will need to be re-subscribed to a different product.

Safer alternatives:
- gcloud apigee products describe: Review product configuration
- Remove individual apps from the product instead
- Set product access to private before deletion to verify impact

structural_patterns.name: gcloud-apigee-organizations-delete
structural_patterns.reason: gcloud apigee organizations delete removes an entire Apigee organization.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an organization permanently removes ALL environments, API proxies, products, developers, apps, and analytics data. This is irreversible and will completely destroy your API management infrastructure.

Safer alternatives:
- Export all configurations and proxy bundles before deletion
- Delete individual resources to verify nothing is still in use
- Consider disabling billing instead if just reducing costs

structural_patterns.name: gcloud-apigee-deployments-undeploy
structural_patterns.reason: gcloud apigee deployments undeploy removes an API deployment.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Undeploying an API proxy stops all traffic processing for that API in the specified environment. Clients will receive errors until the API is redeployed. This is reversible by redeploying the proxy.

Safer alternatives:
- gcloud apigee deployments list: Review current deployments
- Test in a non-production environment first
- Deploy a new revision instead of undeploying

structural_patterns.name: apigeecli-apis-delete
structural_patterns.reason: apigeecli apis delete removes an API proxy from Apigee.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an API proxy removes all its revisions and deployment history. Any applications relying on this API will immediately receive errors.

Safer alternatives:
- apigeecli apis list: Review APIs before deletion
- apigeecli apis export: Export the proxy bundle for backup
- Undeploy from all environments first to verify impact

structural_patterns.name: apigeecli-products-delete
structural_patterns.reason: apigeecli products delete removes an API product from Apigee.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an API product immediately revokes access for all subscribed apps. Quota settings and rate limits are permanently lost.

Safer alternatives:
- apigeecli products list: Review products before deletion
- apigeecli products get: Check which apps are using the product
- Remove apps from the product first to verify impact

structural_patterns.name: apigeecli-developers-delete
structural_patterns.reason: apigeecli developers delete removes a developer from Apigee.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a developer also deletes all their apps and API keys. Applications using those keys will immediately lose access.

Safer alternatives:
- apigeecli developers list: Review developers before deletion
- apigeecli apps list: Check developer's apps first
- Set developer status to inactive instead of deleting

structural_patterns.name: apigeecli-envs-delete
structural_patterns.reason: apigeecli envs delete removes an Apigee environment.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an environment removes all deployed proxies, target servers, and configurations. This breaks all API traffic through that environment.

Safer alternatives:
- apigeecli envs list: Review environments before deletion
- Export all environment resources before deletion
- Undeploy all APIs first to verify nothing is in use

structural_patterns.name: apigeecli-orgs-delete
structural_patterns.reason: apigeecli orgs delete removes an entire Apigee organization.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an organization permanently removes ALL resources: environments, APIs, products, developers, apps, and analytics. This is completely irreversible.

Safer alternatives:
- Export all configurations before deletion
- Delete individual resources first to verify impact
- Contact support if you need to preserve any data

structural_patterns.name: apigeecli-apps-delete
structural_patterns.reason: apigeecli apps delete removes a developer app from Apigee.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an app revokes all its API keys and credentials. Any systems using those keys will immediately lose API access.

Safer alternatives:
- apigeecli apps list: Review apps before deletion
- Revoke individual credentials instead of deleting the app
- Set app status to revoked to disable without deleting

structural_patterns.name: apigeecli-keyvaluemaps-delete
structural_patterns.reason: apigeecli keyvaluemaps delete removes a key-value map from Apigee.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a KVM removes all stored key-value pairs. API proxies reading from this KVM will fail or return errors. Configuration data stored in the KVM is permanently lost.

Safer alternatives:
- apigeecli keyvaluemaps list: Review KVMs before deletion
- Export KVM entries before deletion
- Check which proxies reference this KVM first

structural_patterns.name: apigeecli-targetservers-delete
structural_patterns.reason: apigeecli targetservers delete removes a target server from Apigee.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a target server breaks all API proxies that route traffic to it. Requests will fail until proxies are updated to use a different target.

Safer alternatives:
- apigeecli targetservers list: Review targets before deletion
- Update API proxies to use a different target first
- Set the target server to disabled to test impact
